data.title: OptimizeRx Stock: A Buy-The-Dip Opportunity For This Above-Average Growth Story
data.description:
This article continues my coverage of OptimizeRx (OPRX). I last wrote about OPRX back in July 2025. The company's Q3 2025 earnings results reinforce my long-term bullish thesis for the stock.

The stock recently experienced a profit-taking dip. There is no fundamental reason for the stock to drop much further. The company has been expanding margins, which led to profitability after experiencing losses in the past. OPRX is growing at a strong double-digit pace for both revenue and earnings. The valuation remains attractive, providing further potential upside for the stock.

Insights from OPRX's Q3 2025 Results

OptimizeRx achieved positive earnings results in Q3 2025. The quarter provides insights into the company's future performance. Revenue increased 22% y-o-y to $26.1 million. The gross margin increased from 63.1% in Q3 2024 up to 67.2% in Q3 2025. This margin expansion drove a y-o-y increase of 155% in adjusted EBITDA to $5.1 million.

OPRX's contracted revenue is ahead of last year's pace. This speaks to the company's focus on operational excellence and its efforts to deepen relationships with its customers and partners. The industry is in the midst of a major transformation. OptimizeRx believes that they are "positioned to redefine how pharmaceutical companies, patients, and prescribers connect." The company's culture enables OPRX to "attract, retain, and strengthen relationships" with its partners.

OPRX increased its average revenue per top 20 pharmaceutical manufacturers to $3.1 million in Q3 2025 over the $2.9 million from Q3 2024. The net revenue retention rate remained strong at 120%.

The company's improved cash flow will allow OPRX to continue paying down its debt at an accelerated rate. OptimizeRx also doesn't see a need to access the equity capital markets for the foreseeable future. OPRX had an operating cash flow of $11.8 million over the past 12 months. This is a significant improvement over the $4.9 million in operating cash flow from 2024. The improved cash flow has the company improving its balance sheet while not needing to raise capital with a potentially share-dilutive equity raise.

OptimizeRx has a goal to become a Rule of 40 company. A Rule of 40 company is a benchmark for a software-as-a-service (SaaS) firm to achieve a revenue growth rate plus profit margin to be at least 40%. This is to identify the company as a healthy, sustainable business, which should create value for shareholders. Based on the Q3 results, OPRX's revenue growth rate of 22% plus its net profit margin of 3% gives the company a score of 25, which is just 15 points shy of the Rule of 40. The net margin improved significantly over the negative 43% from Q3 2024. So, continued revenue and margin expansion should get the company to the benchmark of 40 sometime in the future.

OptimizeRx has been "redefining how pharmaceutical companies, doctors, and patients connect, communicate, and act to improve patient outcomes." OPRX's platform integrates advanced patient-finding tools through efficient targeting. The platform is transforming engagement across the health care ecosystem. The company believes that its reach for point-of-care and direct-to-consumer channels provides a sustainable competitive advantage.

OPRX is the only company with the "scale, technology, and data integration to engage providers & patients seamlessly." This enables the company to deliver the industry's most "comprehensive commercialization platform." Customers are supported across the full product life cycle, and client relationships are deepened through OPRX's platform.

OptimizeRx stated that they plan to "further its reach, scalability, and role as a trusted strategic partner" going forward. This goal is to help pharma manufacturers solve some of their most urgent commercialization challenges. This includes: "improving interoperability, enhancing brand visibility, reducing script abandonment, and supporting the growing shift toward complex specialty medications."

The positive momentum that OPRX has been achieving led the company to increase guidance for 2025. OPRX now expects revenue for 2025 to be $105 million to $109 million and adjusted EBITDA to be $16 million to $19 million. This is up from the guidance given at the end of Q2 2025 for revenue of $104 million to $108 million and adjusted EBITDA of $14.5 million to $17.5 million. OptimizeRx also gave guidance for 2026 revenue to be $118 million to $124 million and adjusted EBITDA of $19 million to $22 million.

Valuation

OptimizeRx looks undervalued as it trades at 15.5x expected EPS of $0.86 for 2026. This is below the sector median forward P/E of 19.4x. OPRX is also trading with a forward price/sales of 2.3x vs. the sector median of 4x.

The expected EPS of $0.86 for 2026 would be about 16% growth over the expected EPS of $0.75 for 2025. The earnings growth can be driven by analysts' expected revenue growth of 12.5% for 2026. This growth would result in revenue of $108 million, which is in the upper range of OPRX's guidance. Earnings should also get a boost from the margin expansion that the company has been achieving.

OPRX's attractive valuation leaves room for the stock to move higher as earnings continue to grow. The stock could also get a boost from some P/E expansion.

Technical Perspective

OPRX's daily chart above shows a sharp correction since the end of October 2025. I don't see any reason for the sell-off other than profit-taking after the stock made a strong move higher from the single digits from the beginning of the year.

The stock is now showing a bullish divergence as the price made a lower low while the RSI made a higher low. Bullish divergences like this tend to result in a price reversal from negative to positive, from my observations. The MACD is also showing a higher low, which reinforces the bullish divergence. The MACD also made a bullish crossover as the blue line crossed above the red signal line while the histogram bars turned green.

I expect the stock to achieve above-average long-term growth as revenue remains strong in the double digits, margins are expanding, which are both driving above-average earnings growth.

Risks for the Stock

OptimizeRx has been expanding margins, leading to its current profitability. It is possible that the company struggles to sustain profitability. OPRX could face unexpected increases in costs, which could narrow margins, leading to net losses. Margins could also narrow due to less than expected revenue growth.

OPRX did improve its balance sheet. However, the company still has more total debt of $28.6 million as compared to total cash of $19.5 million. This leaves the company with $9.1 million in net debt. The debt could be an issue if OPRX experienced a sustained period of net losses, reducing the ability to pay off the debt.

Competition could be another threat. Other companies with more financial and other resources could develop an attractive platform that accomplishes what OPRX does. This could take market share away from OptimizeRx and make it more difficult for the company to grow.

OptimizeRx's Long-Term Outlook

OptimizeRx looks like a solid investment going forward. OptimizeRx has an effective platform that connects patients, doctors, and pharmaceutical companies, which is driving revenue growth. The company's efforts at margin expansion are paying off with improved profitability and strong earnings growth.

The recent pullback in the stock looks like a buying opportunity. I expect the stock to outperform the broader market over the long term when looking a year or more ahead. The potential outperformance can be driven by OPRX's above-average revenue and earnings growth. Plus, the stock can get a boost from the low valuation level.

data.title: AZZ: Upside Is Being Left On The Table
data.description:
You cannot always expect the market to move in your favor, especially over short windows of time. One company that has recently fallen short of my expectations is AZZ Inc. (AZZ), an enterprise that provides specialized products and services for the industrial and construction spaces. In my last article about the company, published in September of this year, I reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate. But the stock has fallen since then, dropping about 2.1%, while the S&P 500 is up 3.4%.

This is disappointing to say the least. After all, whenever I rate a company a ‘buy,’ I'm making the claim that shares are likely to outperform the market for the foreseeable future. But the good news is that, since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in April of this year, the firm is doing just that, even if only marginally so. Shares are up 32.7%, beating out the 27.6% rise that the market experienced. Looking at the data today, I see a vibrant company that should continue to grow. Management also has big expectations for the future. Shares are cheap on an absolute basis and are slightly cheap compared to other similar enterprises.

Given this combination of factors, I believe that the company justifies a very soft ‘buy’ rating at this time. Having said that, my opinion could change as new data comes in. It just so happens that, after the market closes on January 7th, management will be announcing financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. At present, analysts believe that revenue and earnings will increase year over year. Assuming this comes to fruition, I see no reason to change this rating right now.

Digging Into AZZ

To be honest with you, the financial picture for AZZ does look somewhat mixed. Take revenue as an example. In this regard, the company is doing a fine job. During its most recent quarter, which would be the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the company reported sales of $417.3 million. This represents an increase over the $409 million the company reported a year earlier. Net income, meanwhile, grew from $35.4 million to $89.3 million. However, the profitability reported by the company has been distorted by certain one-time events. Specifically, during the second quarter of 2025, the company booked only $1.5 million of equity and earnings of unconsolidated subsidiaries. But this year, those earnings amounted to $59.3 million. This disparity was caused by a $109.4 million gain after the company sold its AVAIL Electrical Products Group. This was offset somewhat by a $45.9 million impairment charge that the company recorded associated with that transaction. On an adjusted basis, net profits grew more modestly from $41.3 million to $46.9 million.

Unfortunately, this is where the good news gives way to some more mixed results. We have seen operating cash flow for the company improve nicely, rising from $47.5 million in the second quarter of last year to $58.4 million at the same time this year. But if we strip out changes in working capital and other one-time fluctuations, we actually get a decline from $33.4 million to $9.7 million. Similarly, EBITDA for the company contracted from $91.9 million to $88.7 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time last year. This shows very similar volatility. But I would focus, most say, on the revenue, adjusted earnings, and EBITDA.

To really understand what's going on, we should dig deeper into its different operating segments. The largest of these is its AZZ Precoat Metals segment. Overall revenue for this part of the company contracted from $237.5 million to $227.3 million. This drop of 4.3% was blamed on lower volumes of coil coated products that more than offset higher prices that management incurred and was able to pass on to the customer. Naturally, this decline in revenue brought with it lower profitability as well. But it wasn't just because of the decline in revenue that profits fell from $42.5 million to $36.5 million. It was also because of higher costs associated with a new plant that the company started operations at in the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. So at least some of that decline in profits should be short-term in nature.

By comparison, the AZZ Metal Coatings segment is doing exceptionally well. Revenue shot up from $171.5 million to $190 million. Higher volumes of steel processed by the company more than offset a $2.8 million hit to the average selling price that management blamed on a change in product mix. And with this increase in revenue came higher profits as well, with pre-tax income growing from $47.7 million to $51.7 million. For the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, the segment seems to be doing well also, with revenue and profits rising year over year.

What's really exciting here is that, even though the company is experiencing some mixed results, with its AZZ Precoat Metals segment continuing to be negatively affected by high tariffs and soft demand in the building construction, HVAC, and appliances markets, the overall picture for the business should be solid for this year. Management expects that higher volumes and infrastructure projects should continue to push revenue higher in its AZZ Metal Coatings segment, even though there has been some margin pressure because of higher exposure to solar and transmission distribution end markets. Despite these challenges, management expects revenue this year to be between $1.625 billion and $1.725 billion. For context, last year, the firm generated $1.58 billion in sales.

Speaking of last year, the company generated EBITDA of $347.9 million. This year, we can expect it to be between $360 million and $400 million. Having said that, management did say that it will likely be in the lower half of that range because of the aforementioned asset sale. Adjusted earnings per share should be between $5.75 and $6.25. At the midpoint, we are looking at an adjusted net income of around $181.5 million. Meanwhile, adjusted operating cash flow is on track to come in at about $269.5 million.

Back when I wrote about the company in September, management had been forecasting that revenue would eventually grow to $2 billion or more by the year 2028. In my previous article, I detailed how management expected at least some margin growth. So in that article, I assumed that EBITDA would climb to $460 million. Scaling up the other profitability metrics similarly would give us net profits of $219.5 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $325.9 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the estimates for 2026 and the historical results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are not great multiples, but they are far from bad. And in fact, the company does fit into the modestly undervalued category as I see it. Relative to other similar firms, the stock as it's priced today is also a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. Yes, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, three of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is. But two of them were cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, while one of them was cheaper on a price-to-earnings basis. On the whole, this puts the company a bit toward the lower end of the spectrum compared to other similar enterprises.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
AZZ Inc. 17.5 11.8 9.9
Griffon Corporation (GFF) 75.6 9.8 9.5
Hayward Holdings (HAYW) 25.7 16.2 13.8
Trex Company (TREX) 19.2 13.4 11.6
Tecnoglass (TGLS) 13.9 14.4 8.6
Gibraltar Industries (ROCK) 683.0 10.1 7.7

At the start of this article, I also mentioned that management plans to announce financial results for the third quarter of the 2026 fiscal year after the market closes on January 7th. The good news is that analysts seem optimistic. Revenue is expected to grow from $403.7 million to $420.3 million. And with that should come higher profits also. The current expectation is for earnings per share to decline from $1.12 to $1.33, which would take net profits up from $33.6 million to $40.1 million. And on an adjusted basis, the rise in earnings from $1.39 per share to $1.49 per share would mean an increase in adjusted net income from $41.9 million to $44.9 million. The table below shows not only these expectations, but also historical results when it comes to certain cash flow metrics for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. In all likelihood, if analysts are correct about the trajectory that AZZ is on, we can expect these metrics to grow also.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that the market is underestimating AZZ a hair. To be clear, this is not some home run or grand slam. But it is a slightly undervalued player that is making some positive changes. Shares are cheap enough and the trajectory is positive enough to justify a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: 2026 Market Outlook: AI To Remain In The Spotlight
data.description:
2025: Tech's enduring reign

Before sharing my outlook for 2026, let's briefly recap what is the YTD U.S. equity market's performance across popular indexes and across U.S. equity sectors. We still have a few business days ahead in 2025, but I think that we are unlikely to see major catalysts [no major earnings or news from the Fed] during these days. It means that performance across sectors is unlikely to change significantly until January 1, 2026. Therefore, we can say with high level of confidence that the Technology sector (XLK) is the best-performing sector of 2025. Second spot is occupied by Communication Services (XLC)

These two sectors, XLK and XLC, have been holding the first two spots since 2023 when the AI mania started. The explanation is quite simple as five out of "Magnificent 7" stocks are representing these two sectors. XLK's top holdings are not only Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Microsoft (MSFT) [Magnificent 7 representatives] but other big AI winners like Broadcom (AVGO), Palantir (PLTR), and AMD (AMD). The two largest holdings in XLC are digital advertising giants with AI exposure: Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META). Since revenues and profits of these companies were mostly growing aggressive in 2025, there is no wonder that these sectors continued outperforming.

Another sector that outperforms so far YTD compared to the broader market (SPY) is Industrials (XLI), which was mostly driven by aerospace and defense stocks, which prevail among the sector's top-10 holdings. This outperformance is explained by the current complicated geopolitical environment with escalating tensions, which has led to an increase in the global military spending. In addition, XLI also benefited from the aggressive AI-related capital investments because all these giant data centers are not only about semiconductors but also need a strong general infrastructure around.

Overall, I think that sector-wide performance across the U.S. equity market was quite robust as a few sectors like Financials (XLF), Utilities (XLU), and Healthcare (XLV) delivered double-digit returns and their performance compared to SPY was solid despite underperformance. I want to separately highlight double-digit returns demonstrated by an ultra-defensive Utilities sector as its performance was also supported by soaring electricity demand from AI infrastructure.

Consumer Discretionary (XLY) and Basic Materials (XLB) delivered high single-digit returns, and only Energy (XLE), Consumer Staples (XLP), and Real Estate (XLRE) were close to zero. The fact that there are no big losers with deeply negative returns in 2025 among the sectors underscores resilience of the U.S. economy despite quite challenging environment.

XLK and XLC are primed for 2026 outperformance

During the Q3 earnings season, all the largest investors in AI infrastructure announced that they plan to continue ramping up data center buildout in 2026 as well. All these companies are named among "Magnificent 7" not just for the sake of a beautiful collocation but because they are known for their ability to consistently generate superior returns on invested capital. The largest investors in AI infrastructure are Google, Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), and Meta. For the sake of this exercise I am excluding Amazon because this tremendous company represents Consumer Discretionary sector as its core business is still e-commerce.

As we can see, these three giant AI spenders are demonstrating exceptional returns based on any key metrics. No matter whether it is ROE, ROA, or ROCE, returns delivered by these three companies are significantly higher than their cost of debt or cost of equity can possibly be. What I want to say here is that if these companies are ready to invest hundreds of billions in AI infrastructure within just a few years, the level of conviction of their managers that these investments will pay off is extremely high.

Introducing new AI features will extremely likely further boost cross-selling potential of these giants. I have high conviction because there is strong record of success in cross-selling and up-selling demonstrated by these giants. However, there is another important thing that some people overlook when we speak about AI's potential to drive returns. These giants all employ thousands of people, and I am highly convinced that the vast AI infrastructure capacity that is built out at the moment will be used not only for selling new services but to significantly boost efficiency within these tech giants.

The above table suggests that Microsoft, Google, and Meta collectively employ almost half-a-million people. If we assume that the average annual total cost per employee [including all the benefits beyond salary] is $200 thousand, the cumulative annual employment-related costs for these giants is somewhere around $100 billion. If we conservatively assume that AI will be able to replace 30% of the jobs across these three companies over the next five years, it will mean a total of $30 billion in annual cost savings. This amount is more than 10% of these companies' TTM net profits [a total of $286 billion]. Of course, these savings will not be recorded in 2026 alone because it will take time. However, I shared this mental exercise with you just to show that there is large reserve for future EPS revisions of these companies.

And the beauty for the Technology sector doesn't end there. It is vital to understand that the lion's portion of AI infrastructure investments remain within the XLK sector because there is scarce supply of high-performance semiconductors and chips. When demand outweighs supply, it means that "picks and shovels" sellers like Nvidia, Broadcom, AMD, and Micron are all able to charge premium prices for their products. Since these companies have been historically strong in converting revenue growth into profits, this will be another strong growth engine for XLK.

What about other sectors?

I think that Industrials have solid potential to outperform in 2026 as well as the two major tailwinds that we saw in 2025 are still there. As I said before, tech giants are ready to invest even more in AI infrastructure projects, which means that this tailwind will still be strong for Industrials as well. Moreover, since the geopolitical tensions are not easing and because defense contracts have long-term nature, I expect this tailwind to remain strong for XLI in 2026 as well. Ambitious AI infrastructure plans of tech giants make me bullish about Utilities as well, as electricity demand is poised to soar further.

Growing investments in AI infrastructure and expected strong performance of the Industrial sector is likely to support the U.S. economic growth in 2026. My positive outlook for the U.S. 2026 GDP growth is also based on the Fed's quite aggressive stance for the monetary policy as only one rate cut is forecasted for the next year. The unemployment rate has been climbing up in 2025, but the current 4.6% level is still notably below the long-term average of around 5.7%. I am not a macroeconomist, but I think that these are solid indications that the U.S. economy will continue growing in 2026. With the positive macroeconomic outlook, I expect primary cyclical sectors like Financials, Materials, and Consumer Discretionary to also generate positive returns in 2026.

Since I expect growth stocks [Technology and major Communication Services players] to outperform, I think that the investors' appetite for defensive sectors like Consumer Staples, Energy and Real Estate to be weak. I expect these sectors to be laggards, and Energy looks like the weakest one due to the reasons I mentioned in my recent SCHD thesis. Among the defensive sectors, there might be positive surprises from Healthcare because the GLP-1 market is still strong which made Eli Lilly almost a trillion-dollar company with the largest 15% weight in the XLV sector. Moreover, hefty dividend yields offered by some large pharmaceutical companies might also attract income investors as Treasury yields are likely to moderate further in 2026. On the other hand, there are risks related to adverse regulatory shifts for the Healthcare sector, which might offset the positives.

Calculating the S&P 500 target for 2026

The S&P 500 Index (SP500) currently dances around 6,900, which is quite close to the big psychological level of 7,000. With strong EPS growth expected for major technology companies in 2026, I think that S&P 500 will easily break through this psychological level next year.

To build the forecast for S&P 500's percentage change, I want to look at the expected FY2026 EPS growth consensus forecasts for the largest companies in the index. There are 500 companies in the index and looking at all absolutely all companies does not look like an efficient exercise, especially taking into account that the top-10 companies [by market cap] represent more than 40% of the index. The top-20 represents approximately half of the index, meaning that everything outside the top-20 individually represents far less than one percent.

Therefore, I have gathered consensus forecasts for top-20 S&P 500 companies by market cap and their expected effect on average EPS growth of S&P 500 based on each weight. I applied FY2026 consensus forecasts for companies with their fiscal years matching calendar years and FY2027 consensus forecasts for companies which are already in FY2026 [like NVDA, MSFT and others]. In the above table we can see that even Palantir's massive forecasted 40% EPS growth in FY2026 affects the S&P 500 growth forecast marginally due to its 0.76% weight. Therefore, I think that it is acceptable to assume that EPS growth/decline of 480 S&P 500 companies outside the top-20 will not have a dramatic net effect.

With all that being said, I think that the weighted-average 11.5% EPS growth that I have figured out above is a quite reliable upside potential for the broader market in 2026. Multiplying the current level of approximately 6,900 by 11.5% upside potential results in a target S&P 500 level of almost 7,700.

Risks to consider

I think that we live in an extremely uncertain and fragile environment because the geopolitical landscape is extremely complex with wars ongoing both on battlefields and in international trade. We are still uncertain what the effect on consumer prices will new tariffs have. In 2022, we saw that Mr. Market hates the word "inflation" even though profits of major technology companies continued growing. Another significant growth in inflation might not only compress valuations but can also adversely affect aggressive AI infrastructure budgets of tech giants. As we can see in my above calculations, out of the 11.5% projected S&P 500 growth, more than one-third of it depends on Nvidia's growth. And Nvidia's EPS growth potential will diminish if tech giants cut their AI CAPEX budgets because less data centers will mean lower demand for GPUs.

In addition, there is "Presidential cycle theory" suggesting that, on average, the stock market performs the weakest during the second year of a presidential term. The logic is straightforward but fair here as the weaker second-year performance stems from political realties. Year one is usually strong because presidents tend to focusing on delivering on their campaign promises. But after that, there might be some unpopular measures in year two like cutting large governmental projects or discontinuing incentives supporting some sectors. Next year will be President Trump's second year, and if this historical pattern repeats, we are unlikely to see broader market's double-digit growth.

Bottom line

My target S&P 500 level for the next 12 months is 7,700, implying another year of double-digit growth for the U.S. broader market. Therefore, I think that SPY and all other large ETFs tracking the S&P 500 index are all "Strong Buys."

data.title: Recent Price Drop Brings Chesapeake Utilities Down To A More Attractive Valuation
data.description:
A number of natural gas utility companies have seen their stock prices slide over the last several weeks. Earlier this month, I wrote an analysis of ONE Gas, Inc. (OGS), a company that operates in Oklahoma, Kansas, and Texas. I rated the stock as a Hold, stating that the recent pullback for that stock appeared warranted. I also wrote about Unitil Corporation (UTL), a company that has service operations for both natural gas and electricity. I liked the prospects for that company a little more than ONE Gas, but Unitil has an unfavorable service area and has been issuing new shares to prop up its balance sheet.

Chesapeake Utilities Corporation (CPK) is another company that has been on my radar for similar reasons. Its stock is down about 9.1% in the last 20 trading days. However, the company is slightly different from some others as it has some midstream operations as well as a small electric utility in the state of Florida.

When so many companies in the same sector get hit with the same amount of pessimism, I believe this often creates opportunity. However, with many of these gas providers, it appears that some recent trading actions were warranted as valuations had become stretched. Such is the case with CPK. The midstream operations make the stock more interesting to me, but I am still giving it a Hold rating based on the information presented here.

Company Overview

Based in Dover, Delaware, Chesapeake Utilities is a diversified energy company with both regulated and unregulated operations. Its business divisions include natural gas distribution in Delaware, Maryland, and Florida; natural gas transmission in Ohio, Maryland, and Florida; electric distribution in Florida; and propane distribution in Delaware, Maryland, Pennsylvania, and Virginia. The company also has an energy delivery development segment in Florida. CPK has a market cap of just over $3 billion as of December 19.

Through a combination of organic growth and selected strategic acquisitions, revenues from CPK have increased by over 80% in the last five years. For the first nine months of 2025, Chesapeake has collected $671.1 million on its top line, a 17.3% increase from the same period of time last year. Regulated energy operations account for almost $500 million of that total, roughly 150% more than the unregulated businesses. A history of the company’s revenues dating back to FY 2018 is shown below.

Total assets for the company totaled $3.86 billion at the end of Q3, an increase of 7.9% since the start of the year. Long-term debt for the company has also risen, reaching $1.44 billion as of September 30 after starting the year at $1.26 billion. Even with this increase in debt of almost 14%, shareholders’ equity has still increased 9.2% in the first nine months of this year.

CPK currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.685 per share, with its final declared distribution from 2025 being paid on January 5, 2026. The company has logged 22 consecutive years of dividend increases with no signs of slowing down next year. Annual payout expansion is traditionally announced with the second dividend payment of each year. Chesapeake Utilities currently has a yield of 2.13%, which places it below the median for its sector, but its 10-year dividend growth rate is just over 9%, well ahead of its other utility peers.

Analysis of Chesapeake Utilities

On November 7, Chesapeake Utilities held its Q3 2025 earnings call, which provided some updates on the company’s recent financial results as well as its projections for the next three years. Its Q3 earnings of $19.4 million, or $0.82 per share, missed expectations by 12.8%, or $0.12 per share. These results were $0.02 ahead of Q3 2024. When the first nine months of 2025 are combined, the company’s net income stands at $94.2 million, or $4.03 per share, compared to $81.9 million, or $3.66 per share, in 2024.

The company also affirmed its earnings guidance for FY 2025. The company expects adjusted profits to land in the range of $6.15 to $6.35 per share, which would represent annual growth of 16% at its midpoint. Analysts are projecting earnings of $6.70 per share in FY 2026, an improvement of about 5.5% to 9% from the previous year. The company has provided the guidance shown below through FY 2028. This estimate uses a 10-year compound average growth rate for EPS of 8.4% to 8.7%, placing the company in a range of $7.75 to $8.00 in three years.

As part of its earnings announcement, the company also updated its capital expenditure guidance for the remainder of this year. In the first nine months of 2025, Chesapeake Utilities has spent $336 million. The company raised the top end of its range for capex spending in the full year to $450 million from $425 million. The increase is due to an initial investment of $15 million in starting its Enterprise Resource Plan and a $10 million allocation to system improvements at its Eastern Shore Natural Gas subsidiary.

Chesapeake also gave an update on its liquified natural gas storage project that is well underway in Bishopville, Maryland. Jeffrey Householder, CPK President and CEO, said this project, known as the Worcester Resiliency Upgrade, creates the lowest-cost infrastructure option to deliver energy resources and guard against weather-related disruptions in the southernmost portion of its service area in Delaware and Maryland. Once complete, the storage facility should be online in the second or third quarter of 2026. Further details about this project can be seen below.

One last positive noted for Chesapeake Utilities is its ability to grow margins as demand for its services, especially natural gas, is increasing. Year-to-date, gas demand in Delmarva is up 4.3%. For its Florida subsidiaries, Florida City Gas and Florida Public Utilities, demand has increased 2.1% and 3.9%, respectively. CPK reported adjusted gross margins for Q3 2025 improved 12% in its regulated operations when compared to the same quarter last year. In its unregulated business divisions, adjusted gross margins were actually 13% higher, but this represented only 16.3% of the company’s total for the quarter.

Risks to Consider

The valuation for Chesapeake Utilities has been increasingly stretched in recent years. At its recent PE of 20.9, its stock trades above the average multiple for the utility sector. While its peers trade with an average PEG ratio of just under 1, CPK has a PEG ratio of 1.58 over the last 12 months. This gives the company a much more limited margin of safety should any of its plans for the future go off track. The company is heavily invested in capital projects, with a five-year plan that includes $1.8 billion in total capex spending. Any of these projects could have cost overruns that impact future profitability. A chart of the company's historical P/E ratio dating back to 2011 is shown below.

Most utility stocks are subject to interest rate risk. The companies perform better in a low-interest-rate environment as their dividends become more attractive to income-focused investors. Because CPK has a dividend that is already below average, it is already disadvantaged in this area compared to many of its peers. Higher interest rates could also be troubling for the company, as they could increase the costs of future financing and increase interest expenses. As debt has increased for CPK this year, its interest costs are already rising, even as short-term rates have fallen. In the first nine months of 2025, Chesapeake has recorded $54.1 million in interest charges, almost 6.3% more than the $50.9 million it reported during the same period last year.

The final risk to consider is the ever-constant struggle the company must endure with state regulators to approve rate increases in its regulated business operations. This is nothing new for regulated utilities. What is new is the added pressure from some states to keep energy costs down in light of the added demand from data centers. Many states are starting to launch their own investigations into the root causes of the increased cost of electricity. Future rate increases may not be approved as easily as they have been in the past.

The company did report final results from three recent rate cases and the impact they will have on its profits both in 2025 and 2026. The company received positive results on a case in Maryland in April, followed by two productive outcomes in Delaware in June and October. These cases, plus a final order from an electric case in Florida, will lead to a growth of almost 40% in margins in FY 2026 when compared to FY 2025.

The Bottom Line

After taking time to look back through all the company's recent results, I do think I like CPK more than OGS and UTL, the other two companies that I have written about recently. I see no issues holding on to any current shares of CPK that may be owned, but the valuation keeps me from recommending any new positions. The below-average dividend gives me less incentive to be optimistic for the stock, even as it appears to be relatively well-positioned for 2026.

data.title: Allstate: Recent Underperformance Has Created A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of The Allstate Corporation (ALL) have delivered a total return of 3% since my most recent piece, Allstate: The Bull Case Remains Intact Despite Recent Rally, was published on December 8, 2024. Comparably, the S&P 500 has delivered a total return of 12% over the same time period. That said, Allstate shares have delivered a total return of 56% since my initial Buy rating, Allstate: 5 Reasons Why I Am Bullish, was published on December 2, 2023. The S&P 500 has delivered a total return of 50% over this same time period.

Allstate has delivered solid business performance since my most recent piece and 2025 EPS estimates have increased significantly. However, the stock has experienced valuation multiple contraction as insurance stocks have fallen out of favor relative to other areas of the market with more exposure to AI driven growth. I believe the recent underperformance of Allstate has created a compelling buying opportunity.

Strong Business Performance

In the time since my most recent update, Allstate has delivered four quarters of strong earnings beats. The company's strong performance has been driven by a number of factors including policies in force, growth in attractive markets, underwriting discipline, strong net investment income growth, and better than feared catastrophe losses. The company's underlying combined ratio over the past nine months has come in at 80.4% which represents a substantial improvement from a level of 85.1% during the same period a year ago.

At the time of my previous piece, consensus estimates called for the company to deliver FY 2025 EPS of $19.05. As a result of strong performance so far this year, consensus estimates for FY 2025 EPS have increased to $29.00. Consensus EPS estimates for FY 2026 have also been increasing over the past year but EPS is expected to decline from 2025 levels as underwriting, especially on the auto side of the business, returns to more normalized levels amid a deteriorating environment. Net investment income growth is also likely to be lower in 2026 as rates have been declining.

While 2026 is likely to be a more challenging year than 2025 for Allstate, due to a normalization in loss rates, I continue to believe the company is very well positioned to drive long term earnings growth. Moreover, I see potential for efficiency gains from AI related initiatives such as claims adjustor reviews and actuarial work which could lead to lower than expected costs and thus margin expansion over time.

Valuation Has Become More Attractive

At the time of my most recent update, Allstate shares had been trading at 10.7x consensus FY 2025 EPS. Currently, the stock trades at 7.1x consensus FY 2025 EPS and 8.5x consensus FY 2026 EPS. Comparably, the S&P 500 trades at roughly 22x consensus forward earnings which is roughly inline with its forward multiple at the time of my prior piece. Thus, despite delivering a string of better than expected quarters Allstate stock has experienced valuation multiple contraction on both a standalone and relative basis. Part of the reason for this is that investors do expect a more normalized earnings environment in 2026. However, I believe the bigger driver of multiple contraction is the fact that investors have been more interested in deploying capital to AI themed investments which have risen sharply over the past year. Simply stated, the insurance sector has been out of favor and a lack of investor interest has resulted in valuations coming down despite solid earnings growth.

As was the case at the time of my prior piece, Allstate shares continue to be cheap relative to peers. As shown by the chart below, Allstate is now trading at a substantial discount to peers such as The Hartford Group (HIG), Travelers (TRV) and Progressive (PGR) based on forward P/E ratios despite having similar long-term growth prospects. One important thing to note is that the entire group is now trading at a lower forward P/E ratio than was the case previously. Best in class Progressive has seen its forward P/E multiple contract from 18.1x at the time of my prior piece to 13.9x currently. I view the re-rating lower in sector valuations as partially attributed to the fact that investors have been focused on other parts of the market with more AI exposure. Overall, I view Allstate's current valuation as highly attractive and I see potential for moderate multiple expansion from current levels. Additionally, I view Allstate's low valuation as providing better downside protection than the broader market given less scope for multiple contraction.

Repurchase Program and Dividend Increase

On February 26, 2025 Allstate announced a 8.7% increase in its quarterly dividend to $1.00 per share along with a new $1.5 billion share repurchase program which will remain in effect through September 2026. In my previous update I had speculated that I would not be surprised to see Allstate announce a significant share repurchase program as a way to deploy proceeds from its Health and Benefits Business Divestiture. The company has been actively repurchasing shares and $695 million remains under the current repurchase authorization.

I view the company's repurchase program as a key positive in that I view it as a highly attractive use of capital given the stock's current valuation. To the extent shares continue to trade at the current valuation multiple, and the business performs well, I expect the company to announce an increase in its repurchase authorization in 2026.

Lower Interest Rates Are A Key Risk

One key risk to the Allstate bull case is the potential for a further decline in interest rates. As shown by the chart below, interest rates have fallen by a moderate degree since the time of my previous update. The move lower in rates has been driven by Fed rate cuts which are expected to continue into 2026. Allstate's portfolio has a duration of roughly 5 years and thus has a moderate degree of reinvestment risk. To the extent interest rates continue to decline over the next few years then the net investment income generated by Allstate will decline as well. Over the trailing 9 months, net investment income has been $2.557 billion relative to total adjusted net income of $5.516 billion. Thus, Allstate earnings are highly reliant on net investment income and a decline in rates could pose a material headwind to earnings in the future. That said, I see limited scope for additional Fed rate cuts given where inflation is right now. Additionally, I expect the yield curve to return to a more normalized shape as a positive slope returns to the belly of the yield curve.

Conclusion

Allstate shares have delivered a positive return since my previous update and Buy rating. However, the stock has underperformed the broader market by ~10%. The company has delivered very strong results in 2026 and EPS estimates have risen sharply.

Despite very strong business performance, Allstate shares have experienced valuation multiple contraction as P&C insurance stocks have been out of favor relative to AI themed investments. On a relative basis, Allstate's valuation has become more attractive relative to the broader market than was previously the case. Moreover, Allstate shares continue to trade at a significant valuation discount to peers despite having similar medium to long-term earnings growth prospects. Overall, I view the stock's valuation as providing significantly better downside risk protection than the broader market. I am re-iterating my Buy rating on Allstate shares and I now see scope for moderate multiple expansion from current levels.

data.title: ABM Industries: This Drop Is Unjustified
data.description:
Back in late August of this year, I made the decision to upgrade ABM Industries (ABM) from a ‘hold’ to a ‘buy.’ This decision was not made lightly. However, the company was achieving consistent revenue growth across all of its operating segments. In particular, its Technical Solutions and Aviation segments were doing quite well. Profitability metrics were also getting better, and shares of the company had become attractively priced. But since then, the market has disagreed with my assertion. The stock is actually down 13.7%, while the S&P 500 is up 6.8%.

You would think that fundamentals had worsened. And a couple of them have. On the whole, however, the company is doing a fine job. And between organic growth and acquisition activity, investors can expect that trend to continue. With how cheap the stock remains, I have absolutely no problem maintaining this as a ‘buy’ candidate. And if we see the share price fall much further, an upgrade to a ‘strong buy’ could become warranted.

My Opinion is Unchanged

The newest data that we have regarding ABM Industries covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. These results came out just the other day, on December 17th. During that quarter, revenue for the company totaled just under $2.30 billion. That's an increase of 5.4% over the $2.18 billion the company reported a year earlier. This rise in revenue brought with it an improvement in some profitability metrics. The firm's net profit went from negative $11.7 million to positive $34.8 million. However, on an adjusted basis, there was a slight decline from $55.8 million to $54.7 million. Operating cash flow jumped from $30.3 million to $133.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would unfortunately get a decline from $72.3 million to $56.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company dipped from $125.6 million to $124.2 million.

For many investors, these mixed results might be a turnoff. But when we take a step back and look at the picture for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024, we get a much clearer look at how things are going. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. Revenue, profits, and almost every cash flow metric for the company all improved. The one exception to this was adjusted operating cash flow. It dipped ever so slightly. But honestly, not everything can be perfect.

At its core, ABM Industries is an incredibly diverse company. Even though it only focuses on a few things, which would be facility maintenance services, engineering and infrastructure solutions, and the like, its operations are split between multiple segments. These and details related to them can be seen in the image above. But in essence, we are looking at many of the same solutions from one segment to the next, though packaged in different ways and catering to specific industries.

The largest of these is the Business & Industry segment. And year over year for 2025, it did quite well. Revenue of $4.13 billion exceeded the $4.06 billion reported a year earlier. Management chalked this up to client expansions in both domestic and international markets. Interestingly, the firm seems to be prioritizing acquisition over maximizing the amount of money it can extract from each customer. I say this because it actually strategically cut prices on contract rebids. This is a market that has been heavily affected across the economy more broadly because of the weakness in the commercial real estate space.

High office vacancy rates have proven problematic. But according to management, this industry seems to have stabilized, and the firm now anticipates growth to be more similar to GDP growth moving forward. This is interesting to me because while I know that many companies have shifted away from remote work and back toward office-based work, I do think that the long-term trend here is challenging. But one thing I will say is that, if management is correct and this has stabilized, I would have expected an absence of price cuts. Then again, with management acknowledging that while total company revenue and bookings were at record levels, macroeconomic conditions are uncertain, it seems as though they are taking the position of trying to lock in customers while they can.

This rise in revenue brought with it an increase in operating income from $307 million for all of 2024 to $316.9 million for all of 2025. It is worth noting that not every operating segment saw both revenue increase and profitability rise. For instance, the Manufacturing & Distribution segment reported more mixed results. Revenue popped 4.1%, from $1.55 billion to $1.62 billion. This was driven by an expansion of business with existing clients and new business wins. Strategic pricing in order to capture new wins was part of the picture. But on the bottom line, operating income contracted from $166.3 million to $151.4 million. This is because of those same pricing decisions that the company made. On top of this, certain investments aimed at hiring technical expertise also played a role when it came to bottom line pain.

Fortunately, other operating segments fared much better. The Education segment, for instance, saw revenue inch up from $904 million in 2024 to $922 million in 2025. This was thanks to new business wins. And with this, as well as operational efficiencies, operating income grew from $55.3 million to $67.7 million. But the two most impressive segments were none other than its Aviation segment and its Technical Solutions segment.

Aviation is a space that has been attractive since the end of the COVID-19 pandemic. Air traffic has exploded to all-time highs globally. This has come about not only because of increased trade but also because of additional passengers. So it's no surprise here to see revenue grow 8.3% year over year from $1.03 billion to $1.12 billion. New business and scope expansions with existing clients helped. And some of the company's operations here also benefit directly from an increase in travel volume.

Even more impressive was the Technical Solutions segment. This is the part of the company that provides facility infrastructure services like mechanical and electrical systems, as well as design, installation, and maintenance of microgrid systems involving UPS systems, Power Distribution units, and EV charging stations. Revenue exploded year over year, shooting up 18.7% from $809.3 million to $960.6 million. Organic revenue growth was robust, adding 10.2% year over year. And according to management, this was because of an increase in microgrid systems projects. On top of this, the company benefited to the tune of $68.4 million from two different acquisitions.

These were none other than Quality Uptime Services, which provides certain preventive and emergency service programs for mission critical data centers, as well as LMC FM Limited, which provides facility services coverage across Ireland. Quality Uptime Services was purchased in June of last year and cost shareholders $116.3 million. Meanwhile, LMC FM Limited was purchased in June of this year at a much lower $22.5 million. That purchase price did not include $5.8 million in contingent consideration that might be due sometime in 2027. Regardless of the means of this growth, the rise in revenue brought with it an increase in operating income from $69.4 million to $86.5 million.

Speaking of acquisitions, management continues to make more of them. On December 17th, the same day that it announced financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the company also announced the purchase of WGNSTAR, which operates as a provider of managed workforce solutions and equipment support services for the semiconductor and high technology industries. This is a rather lofty purchase, but I can understand to an extent why management has made the decision to purchase it. The reason why I say it's expensive is because the $275 million translates to 2.04 times its $135 million of revenue. By comparison, ABM Industries is currently trading at 0.46 times revenue on an EV to revenue basis. At the same time, however, the semiconductor and high technology industries markets are hot. And management mentioned that WGNSTAR can be expected to grow its annual revenue at a rate of around 10% moving forward. This will also make the semiconductor solutions portfolio that ABM Industries has significantly larger, with overall annual revenue standing at about $325 million.

This purchase should add about 1% to revenue next year. And that's in spite of the fact that the acquisition will not close until the second quarter of next year. But organic revenue growth is supposed to be even more important, adding between 3% and 4% to the top line for the company for 2026. Adjusted earnings per share should come in at between $3.85 and $4.15. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of $248 million compared to the $215.8 million reported for the 2025 fiscal year. And if we project out other profitability metrics accordingly, this would mean adjusted operating cash flow of $338.6 million and EBITDA of $570.7 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Because of how other similar firms are valued, with the ones that I am comparing it to having issues from a net profit perspective, I'm really only paying attention to the cash flow multiples when it comes to the comparative analysis. Regardless, on an absolute basis, these trading multiples are appealing to me. In the table below, you can see the price to operating cash flow multiples and the EV to EBITDA multiples of five other similar enterprises. What I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, one of the five firms ended up being cheaper than ABM Industries. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, our candidate was the cheapest of the group.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
ABM Industries 8.6 8.1
GFL Environmental (GFL) 14.2 15.9
Enviri Corporation (NVRI) 14.8 13.4
BrightView Holdings (BV) 4.2 7.8
Montrose Environmental Group (MEG) 11.3 24.2
Perma-Fix Environmental Services (PESI) 2,091.8 47.3

Moving forward, one thing that I will say that is encouraging about ABM Industries is that, in addition to the company focusing on revenue growth, it is also emphasizing cost cutting initiatives. In the final quarter of 2025, the company launched a restructuring program aimed at slashing annual run rate expenses of around $35 million. This should be implemented throughout 2026. And most of the costs associated with it were already incurred in the final quarter of 2025, with $13.4 million in total restructuring expenses recognized during that time. The firm only expects another $2 million to $3 million in charges throughout 2026. So hopefully, the projected improvements anticipated for 2026 will come to fruition.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I think that the market is making a mistake pushing shares of ABM Industries down like what we have seen. This is a cheap company that's doing a fine job. Acquisitions and organic revenue growth, as well as cost cutting initiatives, check all of the boxes for me. At the end of the day, I have no problem maintaining this as a ‘buy’ candidate. And in the event that we see shares fall much further, upgrading it is probable.
